chapters.title: A starter kit for job-seeking programmers
chapters.text:
Applying for a programming job can feel like entering a battle where the rules keep changing. In a single interview you have a short window to prove you can think mathematically, write clean code, solve problems on the fly, and match the company's values. Even that is not always enough. You also have to look better than every other candidate in the room.

Most interviewers pick the strongest candidate by comparing people side by side.

It is no wonder that smart, capable people feel anxious before a coding interview. The internet is full of guides promising to reveal what the interview will throw at you. This summary is one of those guides. It walks through the most common questions you might face, and it gives you the kind of head start that companies like Google and Microsoft look for. Read on, and the process will feel far less mysterious by the end.

You may end up ready to apply anywhere. Yes, that is a bold claim, but the material ahead is designed to make it real.

chapters.title: Notes for getting hired at the big names
chapters.text:
If you have your eye on a top company, here are some pointers worth saving. Microsoft loves people who live and breathe technology. They may ask why you want to work there, which is your chance to talk about the products you already use and love. A small tell to watch for, if you meet the hiring manager right after your technical rounds, the technical part likely went well.

Apple also wants people who care deeply about the craft. Research the products, come ready with ideas for how you would improve them, and be prepared for one-on-one and two-on-one interviews. If you are invited to the Apple campus, pay attention to who you share lunch with. That person may end up being your future manager, so the meal is part of the test.

Facebook looks for builders who like to create things, whether that is a product, a business, or a tool. Be ready to show that you can craft something original in any language. After the phone screen, expect to receive a take-home coding assignment, so line up a friend who can review your work before you turn it in.

Palantir is another company that will push you with tough coding problems, so brush up on algorithms and data structures. You may also get a HackerRank test, especially if you are still in college.

Google has a reputation for tricky interviews, but the reality is closer to other top firms than the rumors suggest. Google cares a lot about scalable systems, so come ready to discuss system design and scaling.

Amazon likes to experiment with its interview process, so do not be surprised if your experience does not match someone else's. Expect several rounds, including one with a bar raiser, an interviewer from a different team whose job is to keep the hiring bar high. That interview is usually the toughest, and winning the bar raiser over can decide your fate.

Throughout all of this, be kind to your recruiter. They have the power to push for a second look if your first attempt does not land.

chapters.title: Matching yourself to the right tech role
chapters.text:
The tech world holds many kinds of jobs, from product managers to SDETs to software developers. Each role calls for a different mix of skills, and the more time you spend sharpening the right ones, the better you will do. Here is a quick look at what each role really needs.

Product managers have to balance customer focus, marketing sense, and at least some coding ability. Show that you care about the work and let that energy come through. Expect questions about how you handle conflict and how well you collaborate across teams.

Testers and SDETs, software design engineers in test, live or die by their coding and testing chops. The classic prompt is, write the code for X, then test it on the spot. The coding bar for SDETs is lower than for full developers, but showing strong coding skills will still help you stand out.

Dev leads and managers need to prove that they can prioritize, juggle urgent tasks, and keep teams running smoothly. Interviewers want to see that you can motivate others and take ownership.

No matter the role, passion, dedication, and teamwork are the qualities that come up again and again.

What about start-ups? Their interview process tends to lean on referrals. Even if you do not know anyone at the company, you can reach out to an employee on LinkedIn, send your resume, and express genuine interest. That move is often enough to land an interview. Start-ups also pay close attention to personality and team fit, so be ready to show who you are as a person, not just on a resume.

Many people see the SDET role as the easiest door into a tech company. Once you are in, however, switching to a developer role can be tough. Keep your algorithm and coding skills fresh, and you will have options later.

chapters.title: You and the whiteboard
chapters.text:
Coding interviews are mostly a test of how you think through a problem. The trap many candidates fall into is trying to memorize solutions. Memorization rarely survives a live conversation. A better plan is to train your brain to tackle hard problems from scratch, even when the question feels unfamiliar. One useful trick is to write your solutions on paper before touching a keyboard. It feels awkward, but the practice is worth it. The same goes for testing your code on paper before running it.

Mock interviews with friends are the single best way to prepare.

Here is a step-by-step approach that can help you solve problems under pressure. Listen carefully to the question. The answer is often hiding in the details the interviewer drops along the way. Draw an example on the whiteboard. Make it specific with real numbers, keep it large, and skip the special cases for now. State a brute-force solution, even if it is rough. The point is to put something on the board that you can later improve. Optimize the solution. Look at the details you skipped earlier and try to solve the problem in a different way. The better approach often pops up when you stop forcing it. Walk through the code before you write it. Make sure you understand the algorithm and how you will turn it into code. Implement the solution on the whiteboard. Start at the top-left corner because you will need space. You do not have to write the entire program. A small, clean segment is enough to show how you think. Test the code. You will not have time to chase every bug, so think through what each line is supposed to do and focus on the parts you are least sure of.

You do not want your interviewer to think that you are stumbling over a simple question. Stay calm, talk through your reasoning, and keep moving.

chapters.title: Sharpening your code
chapters.text:
Some topics come up over and over in coding interviews, and they are worth brushing up on. The main data structures to know are hash tables, heaps, vectors and array lists, stacks and queues, linked lists, plus trees, tries, and graphs. The algorithms that show up most often include quick sort, binary search, merge sort, breadth-first search, and depth-first search. The key concepts to understand are bit manipulation, recursion, stack versus heap memory, big O time and space, and dynamic programming.

With that toolkit, you can answer most of the questions you will face. If you slip up, do not panic. Coding interviews are not scored as simply right or wrong. Interviewers watch how you think, how you trade time and space, and how you handle pressure. If a question looks familiar, say so. Owning what you know is a sign of honesty, and honesty reads as confidence.

For extra credit, let your excitement for hard problems show. Interviewers remember candidates who lean in when a problem gets tough.

As for languages, top companies do not really care which one you use, as long as you are comfortable. Even if your interviewer is not an expert in your chosen language, they should be able to follow your logic. Pick a language you can write cleanly, because readable code is easier to grade. Java, for example, is friendly to most tech readers, while languages like Objective C or Scala can be harder to parse on the fly.

The more languages you know, the more flexible and valuable you are.

In every line you write, aim for the following. Correctness, so the code runs cleanly under any input. Efficiency, so you use time and space well. Simplicity, so the code is short and easy to read. Readability, so other developers can follow your logic no matter what language they use. Maintainability, so the code can be reused at later stages of the product.

chapters.title: What to do once the offer is on the table
chapters.text:
When an offer lands, doubt and second thoughts are normal. Should you take it, or wait for something better? It feels like a choice that will shape the rest of your career. To weigh the risk, it helps to look at a few key factors.

Salary matters, but it should not be the only thing you consider. A big number can hide warning signs, so read the offer carefully before signing. Pay attention to perks like relocation help and signing bonuses. Annual bonuses can range from three to thirty percent of your pay, so ask your recruiter what is typical and check with current employees about their experience.

The cost of living in your area is a real factor. A salary that sounds generous in one city can feel small in a place like Silicon Valley. Run the numbers with taxes and rent in mind before you commit.

Think about career growth. After a year or two on the job, you will probably want to move up. Can this company give you promotions, new roles, or job swaps? Will its name carry weight on your resume later? Stability is also worth weighing. A stable company is less likely to lay you off, but it may grow more slowly than its peers. The right choice depends on what you value.

The happiness factor is the one that is hardest to measure but easiest to feel. Do you believe in the product? Does the culture fit who you are? Do the people you would work with seem kind and thoughtful?

Rejection is the other hard part of the process. It can sting, but it does not define you. If the company is one you really want to join, stay in touch with the recruiter, learn from the experience, and try again when a new opening appears.

A no on one interview does not mean you are bad at your craft.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Coding interviews are tough, but tough questions are not a sign that you are not smart or creative. Everyone in the room is wrestling with the same problems, so do not take the difficulty personally. Put in the work, show up prepared, and let your interviewer see the best of how you think.

A small step to take today

Pick up Gayle Laakmann McDowell's full book for detailed solutions to a wide range of technical questions. Practice writing code on a whiteboard, since some interviewers will ask you to do exactly that. If you have not heard back from a recruiter after five business days, do not be afraid to follow up and ask about your application.
